companyCaseStudy.title: How Uber Used H3 to Turn Costly Geometry Into Lightning‑Fast Matchmaking (10x speedup, sub‑ms neighbor lookups)
companyCaseStudy.summary: Uber was doing millions of geospatial lookups every minute for dispatch, pricing and geofencing. Complex spherical geometry queries were CPU heavy and slow — by adopting H3 (Uber’s hexagonal spatial index) and shifting to cell‑based storage and precomputation, they cut geo‑lookup latency from tens of milliseconds to ~1–2ms and reduced CPU for spatial matching by ~70%.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Imagine your core matching service spending most of its CPU doing geometry math: spherical polygons, point‑in‑polygon checks and nearest‑neighbor searches — at global scale. Uber faced that exact problem: millions of proximity and containment checks per minute for dispatch, surge pricing, and geofences. The naive approach didn’t scale.

---

## Problem: Geometry at planet scale

- Use cases: find nearby drivers, check if a pickup is inside a service area, aggregate supply/demand by neighborhood for surge pricing.
- Scale: tens of thousands → hundreds of thousands of geo lookups per second across the globe at peak. Each lookup used geospatial functions (ST_DWithin, point-in-polygon), which are expensive on spherical geometry and did not play well with relational DB indices.
- Consequences: high CPU on backend services and DBs, variable latency (20–100ms per lookup during load spikes), and complex code for handling edge cases and different resolutions.

### The challenge

- Need extremely fast neighbor and containment checks (used inline in request flows).
- Results must be consistent across services (dispatch, pricing, geofences) and easy to query in DBs and caches.
- Must trade accuracy for speed in a controlled way: the system must be fast but not catastrophically wrong about “nearby”.

---

## Solution: H3 + cellified storage + precomputation

Uber designed a multi‑part solution centered on H3 (an open‑source, hierarchical hexagonal geospatial index Uber created):

1. Index geography into H3 cell IDs at one or more resolutions. Each lat/lon maps to a single H3 index (a 64‑bit integer). Resolutions let you pick cell sizes — coarse for city level, fine for street level.
2. Store entities (drivers, polygons, hotspots) tagged with one or more H3 cell IDs instead of keeping to raw lat/lng or complex geometries. Example: a driver living at (lat,lng) is stored with h3_index(res=9).
3. Replace expensive geometry queries with fast integer lookups and small precomputed neighbor sets. To find nearby drivers, expand to the k‑ring of the cell and query the datastore for those cell IDs (hash/secondary index lookup, Redis, or a DB index on the cell column).
4. Precompute containment for expensive polygons: convert service areas / geofences into a set of H3 cells at a chosen resolution and store those cell IDs. Containment becomes a cheap membership check.
5. Cache common k‑rings and precomputed cell lists in memcache/Redis so neighbor sets are constant‑time and memoized.

This lets the system answer "Is point P in polygon X?" by "Is h3(P) in precomputed_cells(X)?" and "Who’s within R meters?" by "which cells are within k rings of h3(P)?" followed by an index lookup.


### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client --> Dispatch[Dispatch Service]
  Dispatch --> GeoSvc[Geospatial Service (H3)]
  GeoSvc --> Cache[Redis / Memcached]
  GeoSvc --> DriverStore[(Driver DB) with h3_index column]
  GeoSvc --> PolygonStore[(Polygon DB) storing cell sets]
  Dispatch --> Matching[Matching Service]
  Matching --> DriverStore
  Matching --> Pricing[Surge Pricing]
```


## Before vs After (representative numbers)

| Metric | Before (geometry ops) | After (H3 + precompute) |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Average geo‑lookup latency (per op) | 20–60 ms | 1–2 ms |
| Peak lookups handled (global) | tens of thousands/sec (stress) | 100k+/sec (stable with caching) |
| CPU spent on geo ops | High (dominated service CPU) | Reduced ~70% for geo path |
| Complexity (code & ops) | Complex polygon math per service | Uniform cell lookups + small libs |


## Why it worked — tradeoffs and decisions

- Hexagons vs squares: hexagons have better distortion and neighbor uniformity on a sphere, reducing boundary weirdness vs a lat/lon grid.
- Resolution choice is important: too coarse -> false positives; too fine -> huge cell lists. Uber chose multiple resolutions for different needs (e.g., res 8 for city aggregates, higher res for local dispatch).
- False positives are handled by a short‑circuit fine check when necessary (e.g., if exact containment is required). For most matching, k‑ring + distance filter is sufficient and vastly faster.
- Caching neighbor sets (k‑rings), and storing cell IDs in DB indices makes lookups simple indexed queries.


## Results & impact

- Latency: geo lookup latency dropped from tens of ms to ~1–2ms in critical code paths. That reduced request tail latency for dispatch and pricing flows.
- Cost: CPU usage for geo computations dropped significantly (example projects reported ~60–80% reduction in geo CPU), enabling higher throughput without proportional server growth.
- Developer ergonomics: teams no longer wrote bespoke geometry code; H3 became a shared library and standard building block.
- Flexibility: service areas, hotspots and historical heatmaps are now simple cell sets — enabling fast aggregation and time‑based rollups.

---

## Practical notes if you want to copy this approach

- Pick the right H3 resolution for each use: aggregation, matching and containment likely need different levels.
- Precompute polygon cell sets offline and keep them in a compact store (bitset, Roaring bitmaps, or just sorted cell ID lists).
- Keep a small exact geometry fallback only when correctness is critical (e.g., regulatory boundaries).
- Watch out for polar artifacts and mapping projections when you choose heuristics.

---

## TL;DR

Turn expensive geometry into cheap integer lookups with a hierarchical spatial index. Uber’s H3 approach made neighbor & containment checks deterministic, cacheable and orders of magnitude faster — enabling real‑time matching at global scale.
keyTakeaways: Replace expensive spherical geometry with hierarchical spatial indices (H3) to turn geometry into integer lookups and k‑ring neighbor queries.
Precompute and store polygon coverage as H3 cell sets so containment checks become O(1) membership tests (plus optional exact fallback).
Choose resolution carefully: use multiple resolutions for aggregation vs matching; caching k‑rings and cell lists reduces latency to ~1–2ms and cuts geo CPU ~60–80%.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
from h3 import geo_to_h3, k_ring

# Get H3 index for a lat/lng at resolution 9
res = 9
h3_index = geo_to_h3(37.775938728915946, -122.41795063018799, res)
print('h3 index:', h3_index)

# Get neighbors within 2 rings (approx neighborhood)
neighbors = k_ring(h3_index, 2)
print('neighbors count:', len(neighbors))

# Pseudocode: query drivers table indexed by h3_index for these cells
# SELECT * FROM drivers WHERE h3_index IN (neighbors)

concept.title: Sagas: How to Coordinate Distributed Transactions Without a Global Lock
concept.summary: Sagas let you implement multi-service business processes that need transactional guarantees — without a global ACID transaction. They replace all-or-nothing locking with a sequence of local transactions plus compensating actions, enabling resilient, scalable workflows across microservices.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

In a microservices world, a single user action (book a trip, place an order, transfer money) often touches several services and databases. Traditional ACID transactions break down across service boundaries. Without a strategy, you either accept inconsistent state, lock resources for too long, or build brittle ad-hoc solutions.

**Sagas** solve this by turning a big transaction into a sequence of *local* transactions, each with a *compensating action* if something later fails. This gives you reliability and scalability while accepting *eventual consistency*.

### Analogy: The Travel Agent vs. The Magic Bank Transfer

- Monolith/ACID is like a bank that can atomically move money and reserve flight + hotel in one vault: either everything succeeds or nothing does.
- A Saga is like a travel agent who books a flight, then a hotel, then charges a card. If the hotel booking fails, they call the airline to cancel the flight. It's more work, but it works across independent vendors.

---

### Two Flavors: Orchestration vs Choreography

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| **Orchestration** (central coordinator) | Clear flow, easier to reason & monitor, simpler error handling | Central service becomes a control point | Complex workflows, need observability and retries |
| **Choreography** (event-driven) | Decoupled services, no single coordinator, more resilient | Harder to reason about end-to-end, risk of message storms | Simple flows, highly decoupled teams |

---

### How it works (sequence diagram — Orchestrator example)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant Client
  participant Orchestrator
  participant PaymentSvc
  participant InventorySvc
  participant ShippingSvc

  Client->>Orchestrator: StartOrder(orderId)
  Orchestrator->>PaymentSvc: Charge(orderId)
  PaymentSvc-->>Orchestrator: Charged
  Orchestrator->>InventorySvc: ReserveStock(orderId)
  InventorySvc-->>Orchestrator: Reserved
  Orchestrator->>ShippingSvc: CreateShipment(orderId)
  ShippingSvc-->>Orchestrator: Created
  Orchestrator-->>Client: OrderCompleted

  %% Failure example
  Orchestrator->>ShippingSvc: CreateShipment(orderId2)
  ShippingSvc-->>Orchestrator: Failed
  Orchestrator->>InventorySvc: ReleaseStock(orderId2)  
  Orchestrator->>PaymentSvc: Refund(orderId2)
  Orchestrator-->>Client: OrderFailed
```

### BEFORE / AFTER: Monolithic Transaction vs Saga

| Before (monolith/2PC) | After (saga) |
|-----------------------|--------------|
| Single ACID commit across resources | Sequence of local commits with compensations |
| Strong consistency (sync) | Eventual consistency (async) |
| Hard to scale across teams/services | Scales across services and ownership boundaries |
| Block resources during transaction | Short local locks only; long-lived process state instead |

---

### Simple Orchestrator pseudocode (Node.js-style)

```js
// Orchestrator: keeps saga state and runs steps
async function runOrderSaga(order) {
  try {
    await call('PaymentSvc.charge', order);
    await call('InventorySvc.reserve', order);
    await call('ShippingSvc.create', order);
    await markSagaComplete(order.id);
  } catch (err) {
    // run compensations in reverse order
    await compensate('ShippingSvc.cancel', order).catch(()=>{});
    await compensate('InventorySvc.release', order).catch(()=>{});
    await compensate('PaymentSvc.refund', order).catch(()=>{});
    await markSagaFailed(order.id, err);
    throw err;
  }
}
```

### Choreography example (event-driven)

- Order service emits OrderCreated
- Payment service listens, charges, emits PaymentCompleted or PaymentFailed
- Inventory listens to PaymentCompleted, reserves stock, emits InventoryReserved or InventoryFailed
- A failed event triggers compensations by services that previously succeeded

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[OrderCreated] --> B(PaymentSvc listens)
  B -->|PaymentCompleted| C(InventorySvc listens)
  C -->|InventoryReserved| D(ShippingSvc listens)
  C -->|InventoryFailed| E[Compensate: PaymentSvc Refund]
```

### Practical considerations & common pitfalls

- *Compensating actions are not inverse of the original*: They undo effects as best as possible. Example: you refunded a charged card, but the merchant fee may not be reversible.
- *Idempotency is mandatory*: Messages, commands, and compensations must be safe to retry.
- *Timeouts & partial progress*: Long-running sagas can hold expectations (e.g., reserved inventory) — plan TTLs and customer-facing UX.
- *Ordering and concurrency*: Ensure steps that must be ordered are coordinated; two sagas modifying the same resource need concurrency controls.
- *Failure modes*: Orchestrator crash, duplicate commands, network partitions — persist saga state and design retry/backoff & deduplication.
- *Observability*: Track saga state, provide dashboards to see ongoing/failed sagas and allow manual intervention.
- *Compensation complexity*: Sometimes the compensating action is complex or impossible; rethink domain model (maybe two-phase commit or redesign process).

### When to use Sagas

- Use sagas when a user action must change multiple services/databases and you cannot use a single ACID transaction.
- Prefer orchestration when you need clear flow control, retries, and centralized monitoring.
- Prefer choreography when you want loose coupling and the business flow is simple or evolves independently.

### Small checklist to implement a safe saga

- Persist saga state and progress
- Make all service actions idempotent
- Design clear compensating actions and test them
- Add timeouts and TTLs for long-running reservations
- Provide monitoring, dashboards, and manual recovery tooling

---

### Quick example: compensation handler (service)

```js
// InventorySvc
const reserved = new Map();

async function reserve(order) {
  // idempotent reservation
  if (reserved.has(order.id)) return {status: 'already_reserved'};
  // do DB insert, etc.
  reserved.set(order.id, {items: order.items});
  return {status: 'reserved'};
}

async function release(order) {
  // compensation, also idempotent
  if (!reserved.has(order.id)) return {status: 'nothing_to_release'};
  // delete reservation
  reserved.delete(order.id);
  return {status: 'released'};
}
```

---

Sagas trade immediate consistency for flexibility and scalability. With careful design (idempotency, durable state, observability, and well-tested compensations) they let you implement resilient multi-service workflows without a global lock.
keyTakeaways: Sagas convert a distributed transaction into a sequence of local transactions plus compensations — enabling cross-service workflows without ACID.
Choose orchestration for clear centralized control; choose choreography for loose coupling — each has trade-offs in observability and complexity.
Make every step idempotent, persist saga state, and prepare reliable compensating actions and monitoring to handle failures.
